stem: Let \( p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_{[[n]]} \) be [[n]] propositional variables. Out of the \( 2^{[[n]]} \) possible truth assignments to these variables, for how many is it true that EXACTLY [[k]] of the \( p_i \) are true?
solution: Choosing which [[k]] of the [[n]] variables are true (the rest are false) is a combination, not a sequence, so the count is \( \binom{[[n]]}{[[k]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is off by one variable in the lower index.
This is off by one variable in the upper index.
This is the total number of truth assignments, ignoring the requirement on how many are true.
This resembles a permutation count and does not correctly divide out the identical 'false' assignments.
This adjusts the correct count by the number of variables for no valid reason.
This adjusts the correct count by [[k]] for no valid reason.

stem: Let \( p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_{[[n]]} \) be [[n]] propositional variables. Out of the \( 2^{[[n]]} \) possible truth assignments to these variables, for how many is it true that AT LEAST [[k]] of the \( p_i \) are true?
solution: Sum the counts for exactly [[k]], exactly \( [[k]]+1 \), ..., exactly [[n]] variables true: \( \sum_{i=[[k]]}^{[[n]]} \binom{[[n]]}{i} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This only counts assignments with EXACTLY [[k]] true, forgetting to include more than [[k]] true.
This is the complementary count (at most \( [[k]]-1 \) true), confusing 'at least' with 'at most'.
This is the total number of truth assignments, ignoring the requirement entirely.
This wrongly excludes the exactly-[[k]] case, effectively computing 'more than [[k]]'.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by one from the correct total.

stem: Let \( p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_{[[n]]} \) be [[n]] propositional variables. Out of the \( 2^{[[n]]} \) possible truth assignments to these variables, for how many is it true that AT MOST [[k]] of the \( p_i \) are true?
solution: Sum the counts for exactly 0, exactly 1, ..., exactly [[k]] variables true: \( \sum_{i=0}^{[[k]]} \binom{[[n]]}{i} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This only counts assignments with EXACTLY [[k]] true, forgetting the smaller counts.
This is the complementary count (at least \( [[k]]+1 \) true), confusing 'at most' with 'at least'.
This is the total number of truth assignments, ignoring the requirement entirely.
This wrongly excludes the exactly-[[k]] case, effectively computing 'fewer than [[k]]'.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by one from the correct total.

stem: Let \( p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_{[[n]]} \) be [[n]] propositional variables. Out of the \( 2^{[[n]]} \) possible truth assignments to these variables, for how many is it true that BETWEEN [[a]] AND [[b]] of the \( p_i \) (inclusive) are true?
solution: Sum the counts for exactly [[a]], exactly \( [[a]]+1 \), ..., exactly [[b]] variables true: \( \sum_{i=[[a]]}^{[[b]]} \binom{[[n]]}{i} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This only adds the two endpoint counts, forgetting every value strictly between [[a]] and [[b]].
This is the complementary count (fewer than [[a]] or more than [[b]] true).
This is the total number of truth assignments, ignoring the range restriction entirely.
This wrongly excludes the count for exactly [[a]] true.
This wrongly excludes the count for exactly [[b]] true.
This is off by one from the correct total.

stem: Consider the formula \( p_1 \wedge p_2 \wedge \cdots \wedge p_{[[n]]} \), built from [[n]] propositional variables. Out of the \( 2^{[[n]]} \) possible truth assignments, how many make this formula FALSE?
solution: A conjunction of [[n]] literals is false unless every one of them is true, and exactly one assignment (all true) makes them all true. So it is false for all but that one assignment: \( 2^{[[n]]} - 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to exclude the single all-true assignment.
This is the number of assignments that make the formula TRUE (there is only one: all variables true), not false.
This halves the total instead of subtracting the single true assignment.
This confuses the number of variables with the count of assignments.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by one from the correct total.

stem: Let \( p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_{[[n]]} \) be [[n]] propositional variables, and consider the formula \( p_1 \wedge p_2 \) (the truth values of \( p_3, \ldots, p_{[[n]]} \) do not appear in the formula). Out of all \( 2^{[[n]]} \) truth assignments to \( p_1,\ldots,p_{[[n]]} \), how many make \( p_1 \wedge p_2 \) TRUE?
solution: The formula is true exactly when \( p_1 \) and \( p_2 \) are both true; the other \( [[n]]-2 \) variables are free, giving \( 2^{[[n]]-2} \) satisfying assignments in total: \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the total number of truth assignments, ignoring the requirement on \( p_1 \) and \( p_2 \).
This treats only ONE of \( p_1, p_2 \) as constrained rather than both.
This is the count for \( p_1 \vee p_2 \) (at least one true), not \( p_1 \wedge p_2 \) (both true).
This confuses the free-variable count with a count of assignments.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by one from the correct total.

stem: Consider the formula \( p_1 \oplus p_2 \oplus \cdots \oplus p_{[[n]]} \) (the exclusive-or of [[n]] propositional variables), which is true exactly when an ODD number of the \( p_i \) are true. Out of the \( 2^{[[n]]} \) possible truth assignments, how many make this formula TRUE?
solution: Exactly half of all \( 2^{[[n]]} \) assignments have an odd number of true variables (flipping any one variable toggles the parity, pairing each odd-parity assignment with a distinct even-parity one), so the count is \( 2^{[[n]]}/2 = 2^{[[n]]-1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the total number of truth assignments, ignoring the odd-parity requirement.
This divides by 4 instead of by 2.
This treats the formula like a conjunction of all [[n]] variables (true only when all are true).
This confuses the number of variables with the count of assignments.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by one from the correct total.

stem: Let \( p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_{[[n]]} \) be [[n]] propositional variables, and consider the formula \( p_1 \rightarrow p_2 \) (the truth values of \( p_3, \ldots, p_{[[n]]} \) do not appear in the formula). Out of all \( 2^{[[n]]} \) truth assignments to \( p_1,\ldots,p_{[[n]]} \), how many make \( p_1 \rightarrow p_2 \) TRUE?
solution: \( p_1 \rightarrow p_2 \) is false only when \( p_1 \) is true and \( p_2 \) is false, which happens for \( 2^{[[n]]-2} \) of the \( 2^{[[n]]} \) assignments (the other \( [[n]]-2 \) variables are free). So it is true for the rest: \( 2^{[[n]]} - 2^{[[n]]-2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the total number of truth assignments, ignoring the requirement entirely.
This is the count for \( p_1 \leftrightarrow p_2 \) (a biconditional), not the one-directional implication.
This is the count of assignments that make the implication FALSE, not true.
This confuses the free-variable count with a count of assignments.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by one from the correct total.

stem: Let \( p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_{[[n]]} \) be [[n]] propositional variables, and consider the formula \( p_1 \leftrightarrow p_2 \) (the truth values of \( p_3, \ldots, p_{[[n]]} \) do not appear in the formula). Out of all \( 2^{[[n]]} \) truth assignments to \( p_1,\ldots,p_{[[n]]} \), how many make \( p_1 \leftrightarrow p_2 \) TRUE?
solution: \( p_1 \leftrightarrow p_2 \) is true when \( p_1, p_2 \) agree (both true or both false): 2 of the 4 combinations for \( (p_1,p_2) \), times \( 2^{[[n]]-2} \) free choices for the rest, giving \( 2 \cdot 2^{[[n]]-2} = 2^{[[n]]-1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the total number of truth assignments, ignoring the requirement entirely.
This only counts the 'both true' case, forgetting 'both false' also satisfies the biconditional.
This is the count for the one-directional implication \( p_1 \rightarrow p_2 \), not the biconditional.
This confuses the free-variable count with a count of assignments.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by one from the correct total.
